name: Meyer (4x4)
description: The Meyer method is an advanced 4x4 speedsolving technique that adapts the Roux philosophy to big cubes. Instead of reducing to a CFOP-style 3x3 solve, Meyer builds a Roux-style block structure after centers and edge pairing. It begins by solving R and L centers, building a 1x3x4 block, solving the remaining centers, pairing edges, and finishing with a Roux-based 3x3 stage including CMLL.

steps.name: Solve R/L Centers
steps.description: Solve the right and left centers of the 4x4 cube. These centers form the foundation for the Roux-style block building that follows.
tips: Use efficient slice moves to avoid disrupting future work
Color neutrality on the R/L axis helps find easier solutions

steps.name: Build 1x3x4 Block
steps.description: Build a 1x3x4 block on one side, combining a center column with paired edges and corner-edge pairs. This is analogous to the first Roux block but adapted for 4x4.
tips: Look for pre-formed pairs during center solving
Build the block efficiently by combining edge pairing with block building
Practice this step extensively as it is the most intuitive and creative phase

steps.name: Solve Last 4 Centers
steps.description: Solve the remaining four centers (F, B, U, D) without disturbing the completed block and R/L centers.
tips: Use slice moves that preserve the existing block
Plan center solutions to minimize move count
This step requires good spatial awareness

steps.name: Edge Pairing
steps.description: Pair all remaining edges while preserving the solved centers and block. This reduces the puzzle to a Roux-solvable 3x3 state.
tips: Use slice-flip-slice techniques for efficient pairing
Try to pair multiple edges per sequence
Keep track of which edges still need pairing

steps.name: Roux 3x3 Stage
steps.description: Complete the solve using Roux method techniques on the now-reduced 3x3 state. This includes finishing the second block, CMLL, and last six edges.
tips: Strong Roux fundamentals make this step much faster
LSE (Last Six Edges) step benefits from practice with M-slice moves

pros: Lower move count than Yau for Roux users
Leverages existing Roux skills for 4x4 solving
cons: Steeper learning curve than Yau or reduction
Block building on 4x4 requires strong spatial reasoning
Edge pairing while preserving blocks can be tricky
